parents.mother_research_notes: Susannah Newton Whittlesey Hamlin (H00142, b. Jan 14, 1716, d. May 10, 1803). Per Whittlesey Genealogy #42 child line: Samuel Whittlesey #42 (I00020) m. Susannah Newton Sept 21, 1743; their fourth child was #135 Roger Newton Whittlesey (HD0097). After Samuel's death in 1768, Susannah remarried Hon. Jabez Hamlin of Middletown — hence her 'Hamlin' display name. STAGE 6Y-11 REVERT NOTE: In Stage 6Y-10 I incorrectly 'fixed' this from H00142 to H00143 (Martha Newton Whittlesey). That was backwards — the two Newton sisters married two Whittlesey brothers: Susannah → Samuel #42, Martha → Chauncey #88. Roger Newton's mother is Susannah, NOT Martha. Reverted in 6Y-11 per book #42 confirmation.

enlistment_record.text: Signed "We, the Subscribers, being convinced of the necessity of a body of forces to defeat certain Wicked Purposes formed by the instruments on Ministerial Tyranny do solemnly engage ourselves and enlist as private soldiers..." Dated at Litchfield. Signed: ROGER N. WHITTELSEY.

open_questions.question: Is Elisha Whittlesey (b. Mar 8, 1783, Southbury CT; d. Jan 7, 1863) a missing child of Roger Newton Whittlesey?
open_questions.notes: Elisha Whittlesey (1783-1863) was born in Southbury CT, studied law under Tapping Reeve at Litchfield CT, moved to Canfield, Mahoning County OH in 1806, served as U.S. Congressman from Ohio and comptroller of the U.S. Treasury. His birth year (1783) falls exactly between documented children HD0105 (Chauncey, b.1781) and HD0106 (Susan, b.1784), suggesting he may be a missing child of this entry. Roger Newton Whittlesey (HD0097) lived in Litchfield County CT — where Tapping Reeve's famous law school was located. If confirmed, Elisha is a gen 6 Hooker descendant, and his 20-year law partnership with Eben Newton (HD2927) — also a gen 6 Hooker descendant — becomes a documented civic_peer cross_connection. Action: Check Whittlesey genealogy for Southbury CT branch; Litchfield County records 1783.
open_questions.resolution: Elisha Whittlesey (b. Oct 19, 1783 Washington CT) is Whittlesey book entry #310, son of John Whittlesey (#123, b.1741 Newington CT). His line descends from John Whittlesey of Saybrook through Eliphalet→Eliphalet→John→Elisha — a completely different branch from the project's Whittlesey line (which runs through Rev. Samuel Whittlesey of Wallingford/Milford). Elisha is NOT a Hooker descendant.

military_service.unit: Col. Andrew Ward Jr.'s Regiment, Capt. Bezaleel Beebe's Company
military_service.notes: Enlisted Jan 21, 1776 at Litchfield for expedition to New York under Gen. Charles Lee. Eight-week term. His musket was appraised as part of the company arms Jan 26, 1776.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight-week militia tour to defend New York 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Roger enlisted at twenty-one in Capt. Bezaleel Beebe's Company under Col. Andrew Ward Jr. and Maj. Gen. Charles Lee in January 1776 — orders reaching Capt. Beebe on a Sunday, the company armed and on the march toward Fairfield by Saturday. The eight-week tour took him to defend New York City, his only documented Revolutionary service.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the peace, Litchfield County, many years
narrative_blocks.body: After the war Roger settled at South Farms in Litchfield County and served as justice of the peace for many years, the position of standing in the rural Connecticut hierarchy. He worked his farm there until his death at eighty-one in March 1835.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of thirteen children with Ann Woodruff
narrative_blocks.body: Roger married Ann Woodruff of Litchfield in April 1775, the same month as Lexington, and they raised thirteen children together. The youngest, George Washington Whittlesey, was born in 1799 when Roger was forty-five; Ann lived until 1825.

parents.mother_research_notes: Susannah Newton Whittlesey Hamlin (H00142, b. Jan 14, 1716, d. May 10, 1803). Per Whittlesey Genealogy #42 child line: Samuel Whittlesey #42 (I00020) m. Susannah Newton Sept 21, 1743; their fourth child was #135 Roger Newton Whittlesey (HD0097). After Samuel's death in 1768, Susannah remarried Hon. Jabez Hamlin of Middletown — hence her 'Hamlin' display name. STAGE 6Y-11 REVERT NOTE: In Stage 6Y-10 I incorrectly 'fixed' this from H00142 to H00143 (Martha Newton Whittlesey). That was backwards — the two Newton sisters married two Whittlesey brothers: Susannah → Samuel #42, Martha → Chauncey #88. Roger Newton's mother is Susannah, NOT Martha. Reverted in 6Y-11 per book #42 confirmation.

Dated at Litchfield. Signed: ROGER N. WHITTELSEY.

military_service.unit: Col. Andrew Ward Jr.'s Regiment, Capt. Bezaleel Beebe's Company
military_service.notes: Enlisted Jan 21, 1776 at Litchfield for expedition to New York under Gen. Charles Lee. Eight-week term. His musket was appraised as part of the company arms Jan 26, 1776.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight-week militia tour to defend New York 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Roger enlisted at twenty-one in Capt. Bezaleel Beebe's Company under Col. Andrew Ward Jr. and Maj. Gen. Charles Lee in January 1776 — orders reaching Capt. Beebe on a Sunday, the company armed and on the march toward Fairfield by Saturday. The eight-week tour took him to defend New York City, his only documented Revolutionary service.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the peace, Litchfield County, many years
narrative_blocks.body: After the war Roger settled at South Farms in Litchfield County and served as justice of the peace for many years, the position of standing in the rural Connecticut hierarchy. He worked his farm there until his death at eighty-one in March 1835.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of thirteen children with Ann Woodruff
narrative_blocks.body: Roger married Ann Woodruff of Litchfield in April 1775, the same month as Lexington, and they raised thirteen children together. The youngest, George Washington Whittlesey, was born in 1799 when Roger was forty-five; Ann lived until 1825.

institutions.institution_blurb: Took charge April 30, 1817 (same day dismissed from New Preston pastorate).

narrative_blocks.header: Pastor of New Preston, ten years, 142 added
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel was ordained at the First Church of New Preston Conn. on December 30, 1807 and served until his dismissal April 30, 1817. Over those ten years 142 members joined the church and 167 were baptized.

narrative_blocks.header: Took charge of Hartford's Deaf and Dumb Asylum
narrative_blocks.body: On the same day he was dismissed from his New Preston pastorate, April 30, 1817, Samuel took charge of the American Asylum for the Deaf and Dumb at Hartford Connecticut. The asylum had opened only weeks earlier as the first permanent school for the deaf in the United States, founded by Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale College 1803, Phi Beta Kappa with Master's
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel graduated Yale College in 1803 with Phi Beta Kappa honors and an A.M. He was licensed to preach the following June 1804, three years before his New Preston ordination.

narrative_blocks.header: Conducted seminaries at Utica and Canandaigua New York
narrative_blocks.body: After his Hartford Asylum tenure, Samuel and his wife Abigail Goodrich conducted 'large seminaries' at Utica and Canandaigua New York. While in Utica in 1832 Abigail began publishing The Mother's Magazine, the early American periodical of maternal Christian instruction she would edit for nearly two decades.

parents.father_research_notes: Family of Goodrich (Ridgefield Conn.). Educated at Berlin Conn.

narrative_blocks.header: Edited Mother's Magazine for nearly two decades
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail began publishing 'The Mother's Magazine' in 1832 while in Utica New York, editing it until about 1850. She subsequently revived it under the title 'Magazine for Mothers,' running one of the earliest American periodicals devoted to maternal Christian instruction.

bio.title: Colonel
bio.photo_notes: Civil War portrait; Brevet Brigadier General U.S. Volunteers (Mar 13, 1865); Chief Quartermaster Army of Georgia under Sherman.

death.cause: Died at Saint Elizabeth Hospital near Washington DC. (Findagrave gives Aug 8, 1873; Whittlesey Genealogy #824 records Aug 10, 1873 — minor 2-day source discrepancy.)

burial.plot_notes: Evergreen Cemetery, Owego Tioga County NY (his wife Maria Louise's family region). Buried beside wife Maria Louise Sweet Whittlesey (d. 1878).

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War Brevet Brig. Gen. USV

education.notes: Studied law at Yale; admitted to the bar July 19, 1845. Per Whittlesey Genealogy #824. Degree: Studied law.

career.organization: The Mother's Magazine and Magazine for Mothers (his mother Abigail Goodrich Whittlesey's periodicals)

career.notes: Instructor to the volunteer Michigan regiments at Fort Wayne, Oct 31, 1861. Enlisted as Captain and assistant quartermaster shortly thereafter.

career.organization: First Division, Twentieth Army Corps (Gen. A. S. Williams commander)
career.notes: Promoted to division quartermaster, 1st Div., 20th Army Corps. Twentieth Army Corps was part of Sherman's Army of the Cumberland and later Army of Georgia.
Promoted Brevet Brigadier General of US Volunteers Mar 13, 1865 for faithful and meritorious service.

career.organization: Army of Georgia (under Maj. Gen. Sherman / Maj. Gen. Slocum)
career.notes: Appointed Chief Quartermaster of the Army of Georgia with rank of Colonel, Aug 5, 1865.
Performed duties of Chief Quartermaster of the Freedman's Bureau through 1870, after his Jul 17, 1867 muster-out.
Appointed Comptroller of the city of Washington 1870; held the office until his death Aug 8, 1873.

military_service.unit: Volunteer Michigan Regiments → 1st Division 20th Army Corps (Sherman's Army of the Cumberland / Army of Georgia)
military_service.notes: Instructor to volunteer Michigan regiments at Fort Wayne Oct 31, 1861. Captain and assistant quartermaster (1861). Division quartermaster, 1st Div 20th Army Corps under Gen. A. S. Williams. Brevet Brigadier General appointment Mar 13, 1865. Chief Quartermaster Army of Georgia (Col. rank, Aug 5, 1865). Chief Quartermaster Dept. of Mississippi (Sept 13, 1866). Chief Quartermaster Freedman's Bureau (through 1870). Mustered out July 17, 1867 with rank of brigadier by brevet.

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied law at Yale; bar admission Jul 19, 1845.

cross_connections.display_label: Compiled 1855 Whittlesey history parallel to Talcott's 1876
Compiled 1855 Whittlesey history parallel to Hooker's 1909
First Whittlesey historian — predecessor to current Hooker compiler

narrative_blocks.header: Brevet Brigadier General by Civil War's end
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Martyn served the Civil War rising from Captain and assistant quartermaster (1861) to Chief Quartermaster of the Army of Georgia under Sherman with rank of Colonel (Aug 5, 1865). He was promoted Brevet Brigadier General of US Volunteers on March 13, 1865 for faithful and meritorious service.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale law, then journalism, then quartermaster at war
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Martyn studied law at Yale and was admitted to the bar July 19, 1845. He published his mother's Mother's Magazine before removing to Detroit Michigan in 1854; in 1861 he enlisted as Captain and assistant quartermaster, rising through the war to Chief Quartermaster of three departments.

narrative_blocks.header: Comptroller of the city of Washington, 1870-1873
narrative_blocks.body: After mustering out as Brevet Brigadier General in July 1867, Henry Martyn continued duties as Chief Quartermaster of the Freedman's Bureau until 1870. That year he was appointed Comptroller of the city of Washington, holding the office until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Compiled the 1855 Whittlesey family history book
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Martyn devoted his leisure to genealogical research, publishing in 1855 the first edition of the Whittlesey family history that became foundational for the 1898 expansion. The compiler of the later Whittlesey Genealogy called his work 'invaluable.'

narrative_blocks.header: Eulogized in Detroit Post by his corps commander
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Martyn died Aug 8, 1873 at Saint Elizabeth Hospital near Washington DC, age 51. His corps commander Gen. Williams published a eulogy in the Detroit Post on August 12, calling him a friend of unmatched conscience and honor.

quotes.text: None knew him but to love him; none named him but to praise.
quotes.context: Eulogy by his corps commander, Gen. A. S. Williams, published in the Detroit Post upon Henry Martyn's death August 1873.

quotes.text: Till now my thoughts had too often found a lodgment in the cold grave; but from this hour, when my fond heart would inquire, 'Where have you laid him?' from within and without proclaims: 'He is risen.'
quotes.context: Parental meditation on the death of Charles Chauncey, recorded in Whittlesey Genealogy #822.

death.cause: Washed overboard from the William G. Hackstaff (California-bound) during a storm while serving as navigator. Per Whittlesey Genealogy #825: 'The guards of the ship were low, and, in giving some order to furl in sail in a storm, he himself was washed overboard, Feb. 7, 1849.'

career.notes: After his father's sudden death in 1842, left his studies for maritime life. Voyaged to Calcutta, made two or three trips to China, and one to Rotterdam.
Washed overboard during a storm while giving order to furl in sail, Feb 7, 1849. The ship's low guards contributed.

narrative_blocks.header: Washed overboard from California-bound ship at twenty-five
narrative_blocks.body: On February 7, 1849 — at the height of the California Gold Rush — Charles Augustus was washed overboard from the William G. Hackstaff during a storm while serving as the ship's navigator. He had given an order to furl in sail when the ship's low guards failed him, ending a brief career that had taken him across three oceans.

narrative_blocks.header: Merchant marine navigator across three oceans
narrative_blocks.body: After his father's sudden 1842 death Charles left his studies for the maritime life — at the time, a position on a New York merchant ship was considered 'a great favor.' He voyaged to Calcutta, made two or three trips to China, and one to Rotterdam before signing on as navigator of the California-bound William G. Hackstaff.

narrative_blocks.header: A soul of fidelity, courage, and religious devotion
narrative_blocks.body: Emily was praised in the Whittlesey Memorials as possessing 'a soul of fidelity, courage, religious strength, and devotion that has few equals.' She married Rev. Lucius Curtis on June 28, 1848 and accompanied his ministry across Connecticut, Wisconsin, and Iowa, never having children.

cross_connections.display_label: Kimball Union trustee while son-in-law was principal

institutions.institution_blurb: Trustee for twenty-six years; secretary of the board for four.

quotes.text: with his strictly honest business principles the people recognized the unapproachable character of the man and trusted their all to him
quotes.context: Family genealogy testimony on Newton Whittlesey's role as Cornish Flats' unofficial estate-settler

career.organization: Church at Cornish Flat New Hampshire

career.notes: Self-taught legal knowledge; called upon by townspeople to settle estates. Per Whittlesey Genealogy #361.
Twenty-six years as trustee; four years as Secretary of the Board. Per Whittlesey Genealogy #361.

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Samuel and Salome (Lee) Robbins of Sutton Mass. (per Whittlesey Genealogy #361 memoranda).

research_sources.page: Entry 361 (under Newton Whittlesey, his first wife)
Entry 361 (under Newton Whittlesey, his second wife)

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Ashel and Mary (French) Bacon of Roxbury Conn.

death.cause: Carriage accident; foot and leg crushed; amputation without restraints; died two days later.

burial.plot_notes: New Preston Conn.; cemetery not specified in Whittlesey Genealogy #315.

parents.father_research_notes: Per Whittlesey Genealogy #315 lineage code: Capt. Stephen Cogswell #315 = (Anna, Eliphalet, Eliphalet, John). His mother was Anna Cogswell (the lineage starts with her), suggesting he was descended via his mother through the Cogswell line. Father not explicitly named in #315.

career.notes: Local militia captaincy. Per Whittlesey #315: 'a strong and courageous man.'

narrative_blocks.header: Carriage accident; amputation without restraints; two days
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen was run over by a carriage on October 27, 1837, his foot and leg badly crushed. He endured amputation without being held or tied to the table but died two days later on October 29, 1837.

narrative_blocks.header: Married three times across three decades
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen first married Anna Camp in May 1791 and had six children before her death in 1810. He married Susannah Whittlesey in December 1811 and had three more, then Rachel Seymour in December 1823 and had one final son in 1825.

narrative_blocks.header: Farmer, mill-owner, captain of New Preston militia
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen ran a farm and a mill in New Preston Conn. and served as captain of the local militia, described as 'a strong and courageous man.' His son Stephen Jr. married Lucy Whittlesey, Susannah's niece — making him father-in-law twice over to the Whittlesey clan.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Stephen Cogswell Sr. (I00432) by his first wife Anna Camp.
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Camp (d. Oct 22, 1810), first wife of Capt. Stephen Cogswell Sr.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Lucy Whittlesey at twenty-five in 1824
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen Jr. married Lucy Whittlesey on April 12, 1824 — niece of his stepmother Susannah Whittlesey, his father's second wife. They settled in New Preston Conn. and had three children.

research_sources.page: Entry 371 (under Lucy Whittlesey, her husband); Entry 315 child #iv

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of William and Hannah (Gibbs) Rea of South Farms (Morris) Conn.

narrative_blocks.header: Double wedding with sister Hannah in 1815
narrative_blocks.body: Abby married Henry Whittlesey on July 20, 1815 — the same day her sister Hannah married Henry's younger brother Frederick. Both Rea sisters became Whittlesey wives in a single ceremony at South Farms Connecticut.

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of William and Hannah (Gibbs) Rae of South Farms (Morris) Conn. (Whittlesey #368 spells the surname 'Rae' here vs 'Rea' for sister Abby — same family).

narrative_blocks.header: Double wedding with sister Abby in 1815
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah married Frederick Whittlesey on July 20, 1815 — the same day her sister Abby married Frederick's elder brother Henry. Both Rea sisters became Whittlesey wives in a single ceremony at South Farms Connecticut.

parents.father_research_notes: Family of Tuller of Avon Conn.

research_sources.page: Entry 372 (under George Washington Whittlesey, his first wife)

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Joseph Boardman of Middletown Conn.

research_sources.page: Entry 372 (under George Washington Whittlesey, his second wife)

sources.notes: Monument inscription preserved; Bacon's Historical Collections I, 239

notable.notable_blurb: Milford pastor thirty years, Yale and Harvard honors

narrative_blocks.header: His father preached his ordination sermon
narrative_blocks.body: When Samuel was ordained at Milford on December 9, 1737, the sermon was preached by his own father, Rev. Samuel Whittelsey of Wallingford — and then published, the elder man’s fourth and final printed work.

narrative_blocks.header: A Latin stone, translated for the curious
narrative_blocks.body: His Milford monument, in Latin, credits him with honors from both Yale and Harvard and “virtues, piety and good deeds” that “everywhere shone with peculiar lustre” across more than thirty years in the town’s pulpit.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Milford minister, then Middletown statesman
narrative_blocks.body: Her first husband Rev. Samuel Whittlesey served Milford for thirty-plus years until his death in 1768. She remarried Hon. Jabez Hamlin of Middletown — described as 'distinguished in civil and military life.'

narrative_blocks.header: Whittlesey genealogy: 'lady of high intellectual qualities'
narrative_blocks.body: The Whittlesey genealogy describes her as 'a lady of high intellectual and moral qualities.' This was an unusual tribute for a colonial woman, whose lives were typically invisible in family records of the era.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived both husbands; died at 87 in Middletown
narrative_blocks.body: She outlived her first husband Rev. Samuel Whittlesey by 35 years and her second husband Jabez Hamlin as well. She died at Middletown in 1803, age 87.

notable.notable_blurb: Wallingford pastor, Yale trustee, Old Light leader

narrative_blocks.header: He preached his own ordination sermon
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained colleague pastor at Wallingford on May 17, 1710, Whittelsey preached the ordination sermon himself, from 2 Corinthians iv:7 — and stayed in that one pulpit until his death forty-two years later.

narrative_blocks.header: “One of the greatest men in Connecticut”
narrative_blocks.body: So Rev. Dr. Charles Chauncy of Boston remembered him in 1768, adding that no one he knew could express difficult thoughts with such clearness and ease. Ezra Stiles, later Yale’s president, drew up the printed character of him that ran in the Boston Postboy.

narrative_blocks.header: Fourteen times more in servants than books
narrative_blocks.body: His probate inventory showed an estate of about £22,000 — a scant hundred pounds of it in books, and fourteen times that amount in enslaved and mulatto servants. The leading Old Light voice of Connecticut was also one of its larger slaveholders.

children_summary.notes: No stars in Edward Hooker despite distinguished career — likely incomplete records for this branch. Birth dates for Caroline Newton children all required correction as given dates predate parents' 1712 marriage.

political_offices.title: Judge, County Court
political_offices.discrepancy_note: EH gives 34 years; Families of Early Milford gives 33 years. Unresolved.

political_offices.title: Chief Judge
political_offices.discrepancy_note: EH gives 34 years; Families of Early Milford gives 33 years. Unresolved.

sources.notes: Entry 13.5; birth dates for several children required correction

burial.plot_notes: Buried next to wife Susannah. Tombstone inscription preserved below.

narrative_blocks.header: He commanded Connecticut’s troops at Louisbourg
narrative_blocks.body: Commander of Connecticut troops at the Siege of Louisbourg 1745 — capture of French fortress at Cape Breton Island, one of most significant colonial military victories

narrative_blocks.header: Colonel of the Second Connecticut Regiment
narrative_blocks.body: Colonel of the Second Connecticut Regiment

narrative_blocks.header: Chief Judge of County Court 1737-1771 — 34 years
narrative_blocks.body: Chief Judge of County Court 1737-1771 — 34 years

narrative_blocks.header: Assistant of Colony of Connecticut 1738-1771 — 33 years
narrative_blocks.body: Assistant of Colony of Connecticut 1738-1771 — 33 years

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard graduate; Captain in Queen Anne's War 1709
narrative_blocks.body: Harvard graduate; Captain in Queen Anne's War 1709

narrative_blocks.header: “Newton as steel inflexible from right”
narrative_blocks.body: His Milford epitaph closes in verse: “The part the Hero left beneath the skies / Newton as steel inflexible from right / In Faith, in Law, in Equity, in Fight.” He had been thirty-three years a judge and an Assistant of the colony when he died in his eighty-seventh year.

education.notes: Harvard graduate; year not recorded in sources

military_service.role: Commander of Connecticut troops at Siege of Louisbourg
military_service.notes: New England forces captured the French fortress at Cape Breton Island without British regular army support. One of most significant colonial military victories. Many years Colonel of the Second Connecticut Regiment.

bio.title: Colonel
bio.bio_blurb: Louisbourg commander, judge, Assistant thirty-three years

bio.discrepancy_note: Edward Hooker gives primary surname as Bryan; a second account gives Baldwin. Both accounts agree on father Samuel and mother Martha (Whiting). Bryan preferred as primary account.
